topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The invitation felt like a trap. Leo smoothed the lapel of his borrowed blazer, the fabric suddenly too stiff against his skin. He’d been dreading this meeting with Marcus, the architect of his middle school misery. Marcus, who used to relish the sting of Leo’s awkwardness with a casual cruelty that now, inexplicably, seemed layered with careful empathy. The office smelled of sandalwood and old paper, a stark contrast to the dusty locker rooms of his youth. When Marcus finally smiled—a soft, practiced curve of the lips that didn't quite reach his eyes—Leo felt a sudden, sharp clench in his chest. He kept fiddling with the cuff of his shirt, his gaze darting toward the closed door, searching for an escape route that didn't exist. "So," Marcus began, his voice low and measured, "tell me about what you're carrying." Leo swallowed hard, his mouth suddenly dry. He could feel the weight of every embarrassing moment, every whispered insult, pressing down on his shoulders. It was a strange sensation, this quiet understanding emanating from the person who had once made his skin crawl.

[story 2]

I walked into the consultation room expecting confrontation. Instead, Marcus was sitting behind a large wooden desk, a notepad open before him, his posture relaxed yet intensely focused. The silence stretched, thick and heavy, punctuated only by the faint hum of the ventilation system. When he finally looked up, his eyes were startlingly clear, devoid of the sharp mockery I remembered. It was a disconcerting shift. "Come in, sit down," he murmured, gesturing toward the armchair opposite. My hands started to sweat, the clamminess creeping up my palms. I sat down, trying to anchor myself, but my legs felt unsteady. "I don't know what to say," I managed, my voice sounding thin and reedy. Marcus merely nodded, a slow, deliberate motion. "That's perfectly alright," he said, leaning forward just slightly. The proximity made my breath catch. I found myself staring at the pattern on his tie, cataloging every minute detail, desperate to find some sign that this transformation was a facade, a trick I hadn't anticipated.

[story 3]

The first session was an exercise in forced composure. Sarah watched Marcus as he patiently guided her through a narrative of past hurts. He spoke of vulnerability and coping mechanisms with an authority that was both soothing and deeply unsettling. Every time he made eye contact, Sarah felt a strange tightening in her throat, a physical reaction she couldn't control. She noticed how he handled her silences—never rushing, always allowing the space to expand until she felt compelled to fill it. Later, as she was gathering her things, she caught his eye across the room. There was a flicker there, a shadow of the old antagonism, quickly smoothed over by an expression of profound, almost painful concern. Sarah gripped her handbag until her knuckles were white. The air in the room felt charged, as if the very atmosphere was vibrating with the history they shared, and she couldn't shake the feeling that she was walking on thin ice.
